content:
{
  "title": "Fact-Checking Chain-of-Query Prompting to Improve LLM Factuality",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often generate fluent but factually incorrect ('hallucinated') outputs when prompted with open-ended questions. Standard chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting can amplify errors by reinforcing internal misconceptions.",
  "motivation": "Improving factual accuracy is critical for trustworthy LLM applications in science, healthcare, and education. Existing prompting techniques often do not explicitly verify intermediate steps for factual soundness, leaving hallucinations unchecked. Advances in prompt engineering open new avenues to systematically reduce factual errors through self-verification.",
  "method": "We propose 'Fact-Checking Chain-of-Query' (FCCoQ) prompting: after each intermediate reasoning step in CoT, the prompt inserts a follow-up query that explicitly asks the model to fact-check the just-produced step against a trusted reference (e.g., Wikipedia snippet or retrieval-augmented context). The model must then either affirm, revise, or flag the step as unreliable before proceeding. This process continues recursively, enforcing explicit verification at each stage of reasoning.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate FCCoQ on a subset of TruthfulQA and PopQA, comparing it to standard CoT and retrieval-augmented CoT prompts. Metrics include factuality (verified against references) and answer accuracy. Human and automated evaluations will assess whether FCCoQ reduces hallucination rates and improves overall trustworthiness."
}
